all-time.title: Hottest Board Games
all-time.description: The definitive list — the games worth owning, period. Ranked by a combination of critical acclaim, community consensus, Amazon buyer ratings, and commercial staying power.

games.title: Gloomhaven
games.blurb: The most ambitious board game ever published — a 95-scenario legacy dungeon-crawl where characters permanently retire and the story world evolves from your choices. It held #1 on BoardGameGeek for years. Nothing else at this scope exists in the hobby.

games.title: Pandemic Legacy: Season 1
games.blurb: Twelve months of pandemic campaigns where your failures have permanent consequences — cities get stickers, characters get scars, rules change mid-game. The most acclaimed legacy game ever made and the one that made the legacy format mainstream.

games.title: Spirit Island
games.blurb: Play as nature spirits defending a colonized island. Every spirit has a radically different power set and playstyle; the interaction between them at higher player counts creates emergent strategies that no designer could have fully predicted.

games.title: Terraforming Mars
games.blurb: The flagship engine-building strategy game. Manage oxygen, temperature, and ocean coverage across hundreds of project cards while corporations compete to claim Mars. Long, complex, and deeply satisfying in a way few games match.

games.title: Wingspan
games.blurb: The game that convinced skeptics board games had grown up. Build a bird sanctuary by chaining habitat powers into an engine that feels different every play. Won the Kennerspiel des Jahres 2019 and hasn't left the bestseller charts since.

games.title: Scythe
games.blurb: Dieselpunk Eastern Europe, 1920s. Build mechs, harvest resources, and expand territory in a game where combat is a threat more often than a reality. Stunning production values and a strategic depth that rewards patient, long-horizon play.

games.title: Root
games.blurb: Four asymmetric factions — cats, birds, woodland creatures, and vagabonds — each playing by entirely different rules on the same board. The faction matchups create wildly different games every session. One of the most innovative designs of the last decade.

games.title: Azul
games.blurb: Tile-drafting so tactically rich that tournament players study it. Drafting what you need while denying your opponent what they need is the entire game — and it works in 30 minutes with anyone from an 8-year-old to a chess grandmaster.

games.title: 7 Wonders Duel
games.blurb: Dense civilization strategy in 30 minutes — the rare case where the 2-player spinoff outshines the original. Three distinct win conditions mean every game ends differently, and the tension of knowing your opponent might win on science never lets up.

games.title: Carcassonne: Hunters & Gatherers
games.blurb: The prehistoric reimagining that many players argue is the best version of Carcassonne. Rivers score differently, forests replace cities, and the overall flow feels more cohesive. Teach it in two minutes; play it for years.

games.title: Sky Team
games.blurb: Two pilots land a plane without talking during critical phases. Place dice in shared slots, manage speed and flaps in real time, don't crash. Voted Game of the Year 2024 — the most innovative cooperative design in years.

games.title: Cascadia
games.blurb: Draft terrain hexes and wildlife tokens to build a Pacific Northwest ecosystem. The puzzle of fitting animals into their scoring patterns against a ticking token supply is deeply satisfying — calm on the surface, genuinely strategic underneath.

games.title: Splendor Duel
games.blurb: The tighter, more tactical two-player take on gem-collecting engine building. Three asymmetric win conditions and a gem board you share and fight over makes every turn a negotiation with your own long-term strategy.

games.title: Mysterium
games.blurb: One player is a ghost communicating only through abstract vision cards; others are psychics interpreting those visions as murder clues. The gap between what the ghost means and what the psychics understand is where all the comedy and tension lives.

games.title: Pandemic
games.blurb: The cooperative game that defined the genre. Race to cure four diseases before outbreaks cascade out of control — and they will cascade, because the board state shifts dramatically with every draw. Nothing beats the "we almost had it" feeling when you lose.

games.title: Codenames
games.blurb: The spymaster's constraint — one word must connect multiple targets — makes this a masterclass in lateral thinking. The whole table argues about every clue. One of the best games ever designed for a mixed group of any size.

games.title: 7 Wonders
games.blurb: Simultaneous card-drafting lets seven people finish a full civilization game in 30 minutes. Each player pursues their wonder's unique strategy while watching what the neighbors are building. Plays in the same time regardless of player count.

games.title: Ticket to Ride Europe
games.blurb: The European map introduces tunnels (commit to a route that might cost extra) and ferries (require locomotive wildcards). The added uncertainty makes the routing decisions more interesting than the original — many players' preferred version of the game.

games.title: Ticket to Ride
games.blurb: Claim railway routes across North America before rivals block the paths you need. Elegant enough to teach anyone in 10 minutes, tense enough that adults play it seriously. The definitive gateway strategy game for families.

games.title: Catan
games.blurb: The game that launched the modern board game renaissance. Negotiate, trade, and build settlements on a modular island that's different every game. Its flaws are real — longest game ever when someone leads — but its cultural impact makes it undeniably essential.

games.title: Dixit
games.blurb: Describe a dreamlike illustration with a clue that's neither too obvious nor too cryptic. The clue-giver scores only if some players guess right — if everyone gets it, no points. Beautiful, cross-generational, and consistently delightful.

games.title: King of Tokyo
games.blurb: Roll Yahtzee-style dice, smash Tokyo, absorb damage, buy power cards. The risk calculus of staying in the monster-infested city long enough to score points but not so long you get eliminated is genuinely interesting in a 30-minute game.

games.title: Coup
games.blurb: Bluff your way through a corrupt dystopian court in 15 minutes. Everyone has two hidden influence cards; claim any power you want and dare someone to call you out. The best "one more game" game ever made.

games.title: Camel Up
games.blurb: Camels race around a track while physically stacking on each other — the one on top carries the rest. Bet on the leg winner, the race winner, and the loser simultaneously. Gleefully chaotic, statistically fascinating, and always funny.

games.title: Skull
games.blurb: Three rules. Infinite mind games. Place flowers and skulls face-down, then bet on how many tiles you can flip without hitting a skull. The brilliance is that you know where your own skulls are, and so does everyone watching you.

games.title: Wavelength
games.blurb: A dial with a hidden target on a spectrum (like "hot ↔ cold"). Give a clue placing your clue ON the spectrum; your team argues where on the dial the target landed. The debate is the game and it's reliably funnier than it sounds.

games.title: Telestrations
games.blurb: Telephone meets Pictionary. Draw a phrase, pass it; the next person writes what they think was drawn; the next draws what was written. By the last player, 'mermaid' has become 'toilet surfboard'. Produces some of the funniest moments in gaming.

games.title: Othello
games.blurb: One of the most studied abstract strategy games in history. Flip your opponent's discs by surrounding them — simple to learn in two minutes, pursued at competitive levels with opening theory as deep as chess by those who go down the rabbit hole.

games.title: Hive Pocket
games.blurb: No board — the tiles ARE the board, and it expands as you place pieces. A pure abstract strategy game about surrounding the opponent's queen bee, playable on any flat surface, that improves meaningfully with every session.

games.title: Lost Cities: The Card Game
games.blurb: Play cards to five expeditions — but each expedition only scores if you've committed enough to it. Start one too early and you're committed to a losing path; start too late and the race is over. Compact, tense, and impossible to play just once.

solo.title: Hottest Solo Board Games
solo.description: Games that are genuinely great played alone — dedicated solo modes, automa opponents, or co-ops that shine one-player. Nothing here that can't really be played solo.

games.title: Wingspan
games.blurb: The game that convinced skeptics board games had grown up. Build a bird sanctuary by chaining habitat powers into an engine that feels different every play. Won the Kennerspiel des Jahres 2019 and hasn't left the bestseller charts since.

games.title: Pandemic
games.blurb: The cooperative game that defined the genre. Race to cure four diseases before outbreaks cascade out of control — and they will cascade, because the board state shifts dramatically with every draw. Nothing beats the "we almost had it" feeling when you lose.

games.title: Spirit Island
games.blurb: Play as nature spirits defending a colonized island. Every spirit has a radically different power set and playstyle; the interaction between them at higher player counts creates emergent strategies that no designer could have fully predicted.

games.title: Scythe
games.blurb: Dieselpunk Eastern Europe, 1920s. Build mechs, harvest resources, and expand territory in a game where combat is a threat more often than a reality. Stunning production values and a strategic depth that rewards patient, long-horizon play.

games.title: Gloomhaven
games.blurb: The most ambitious board game ever published — a 95-scenario legacy dungeon-crawl where characters permanently retire and the story world evolves from your choices. It held #1 on BoardGameGeek for years. Nothing else at this scope exists in the hobby.

games.title: Sagrada
games.blurb: Draft colorful dice to fill stained glass window boards, following color and shade placement rules. The shared dice pool means everyone affects everyone else's plans. Beautiful on the table and mechanically elegant.

games.title: Viticulture Essential Edition
games.blurb: Run a Tuscan vineyard through the seasons — plant, harvest, age wine, and fulfill orders. The worker placement is clean; the seasonal rhythm is satisfying; the solo Automa opponent is one of the best AI systems in the hobby.

games.title: Parks
games.blurb: Hike trails through stunning national park artwork, collecting resources and visiting parks before the seasons change. One of the most beautiful games on the market, with enough strategic decision-making to keep experienced players engaged.

2p.title: Hottest 2-Player Board Games
2p.description: Games designed for exactly two players, or that genuinely shine at two — not just "supports 2 players" buried in the rulebook. Strictly-for-two designs are noted.

games.title: Ticket to Ride
games.blurb: Claim railway routes across North America before rivals block the paths you need. Elegant enough to teach anyone in 10 minutes, tense enough that adults play it seriously. The definitive gateway strategy game for families.

games.blurb: Tile-drafting so tactically rich that tournament players study it. Drafting what you need while denying your opponent what they need is the entire game — and it works in 30 minutes with anyone from an 8-year-old to a chess grandmaster.

games.title: Sky Team
games.blurb: Two pilots land a plane without talking during critical phases. Place dice in shared slots, manage speed and flaps in real time, don't crash. Voted Game of the Year 2024 — the most innovative cooperative design in years.

games.title: Everdell Duo
games.blurb: The beloved forest city-builder rebuilt specifically for two, with its own asymmetric storyline and exclusive cards. The charming woodland theme meets real engine-building depth in a purpose-made two-player experience.

games.title: Lost Cities: The Card Game
games.blurb: Play cards to five expeditions — but each expedition only scores if you've committed enough to it. Start one too early and you're committed to a losing path; start too late and the race is over. Compact, tense, and impossible to play just once.

games.title: Quoridor
games.blurb: Move your pawn to the other side while placing walls to block your opponent — but you can never fully seal them off. A pure spatial abstract that's approachable in minutes and studied seriously by players who pursue it.

games.title: Sagrada
games.blurb: Draft colorful dice to fill stained glass window boards, following color and shade placement rules. The shared dice pool means everyone affects everyone else's plans. Beautiful on the table and mechanically elegant.

games.title: Splendor Duel
games.blurb: The tighter, more tactical two-player take on gem-collecting engine building. Three asymmetric win conditions and a gem board you share and fight over makes every turn a negotiation with your own long-term strategy.

games.title: Lord of the Rings: Duel for Middle-Earth
games.blurb: Light and Shadow vie for control across iconic Middle-Earth locations in a strategic push-and-pull card game. The gorgeous production does justice to the license, and the asymmetric factions play noticeably differently.

games.title: Codenames Duet
games.blurb: The cooperative version where both players act as spymaster simultaneously. Your clues must contact your partner's agents and avoid theirs — since you have different information, miscommunication is guaranteed and hilarious.

games.title: Patchwork
games.blurb: Draft polyomino patches to fill your personal quilt, balancing button income against empty spaces. A 30-minute puzzle game that's genuinely deep — every placement has downstream consequences you only see after the fact.

games.title: Hanabi
games.blurb: Hold your cards facing OUT — you can't see them, only your teammates can. Give limited hints to help the group play fireworks in the right order. A cooperative memory game that's harder than it looks and deeply satisfying when it clicks.

3p.title: Hottest 3-Player Board Games
3p.description: Games that hit their stride at exactly three — no empty seats, no ganging-up dynamic, just the right number of players competing for the same space and resources.

games.title: Catan

games.title: Mysterium
games.blurb: One player is a ghost communicating only through abstract vision cards; others are psychics interpreting those visions as murder clues. The gap between what the ghost means and what the psychics understand is where all the comedy and tension lives.

4p.title: Hottest 4-Player Board Games
4p.description: Four players is the sweet spot for most modern board games — full interaction, real competition, and no dead seats. Every game here genuinely shines with exactly four at the table.

games.title: Exploding Kittens
games.blurb: Draw a card. If it explodes, you're out — unless you have a defuse card. Use action cards to skip, shuffle, or steal your way to survival. One of the most-funded Kickstarters ever and still a reliable 15-minute laugh.

games.title: Catan
games.blurb: The game that launched the modern board game renaissance. Negotiate, trade, and build settlements on a modular island that's different every game. Its flaws are real — longest game ever when someone leads — but its cultural impact makes it undeniably essential.

games.title: Ticket to Ride
games.blurb: Claim railway routes across North America before rivals block the paths you need. Elegant enough to teach anyone in 10 minutes, tense enough that adults play it seriously. The definitive gateway strategy game for families.

games.title: Wavelength
games.blurb: A dial with a hidden target on a spectrum (like "hot ↔ cold"). Give a clue placing your clue ON the spectrum; your team argues where on the dial the target landed. The debate is the game and it's reliably funnier than it sounds.

games.title: The Chameleon
games.blurb: One player secretly is the Chameleon — they don't know the secret word but must bluff their way through. Give a clue that's not too obvious (the Chameleon will copy you) but not so cryptic that others suspect YOU. Perfect 15-minute opener.

games.title: Codenames

games.title: Coup

games.title: Carcassonne: Hunters & Gatherers

games.title: Ticket to Ride Europe

games.title: Pandemic
games.blurb: The cooperative game that defined the genre. Race to cure four diseases before outbreaks cascade out of control — and they will cascade, because the board state shifts dramatically with every draw. Nothing beats the "we almost had it" feeling when you lose.

games.title: Monikers
games.blurb: Three rounds with the same card deck: describe it any way, then only one word, then only gestures. By round three everyone remembers the failed descriptions from round one and loses it entirely. One of the funniest party games ever made.

games.title: Chronology
games.blurb: Build a personal timeline by correctly placing historical events in chronological order. Your timeline grows longer (and easier to place into) as you succeed — a clever self- balancing mechanic that keeps everyone competitive regardless of history knowledge.

games.title: That's Not a Hat
games.blurb: Memory meets bluffing — pass gifts around a circle, but nobody can look at what they don't hold. Accept a gift you think doesn't match what you were told, or call out the bluffer. Short, loud, and great at any age.

games.title: Terraforming Mars
games.blurb: The flagship engine-building strategy game. Manage oxygen, temperature, and ocean coverage across hundreds of project cards while corporations compete to claim Mars. Long, complex, and deeply satisfying in a way few games match.

games.title: Cascadia
games.blurb: Draft terrain hexes and wildlife tokens to build a Pacific Northwest ecosystem. The puzzle of fitting animals into their scoring patterns against a ticking token supply is deeply satisfying — calm on the surface, genuinely strategic underneath.

games.title: Trekking the National Parks
games.blurb: Race your hikers across a gorgeous map of the US national parks, collecting trail cards and claiming sites before rivals. Accessible to families while offering real strategic decisions about when to race versus when to stockpile.

games.title: King of Tokyo
games.blurb: Roll Yahtzee-style dice, smash Tokyo, absorb damage, buy power cards. The risk calculus of staying in the monster-infested city long enough to score points but not so long you get eliminated is genuinely interesting in a 30-minute game.

games.title: Disney Villainous
games.blurb: Each player is an iconic Disney villain pursuing their own unique objective on their own board — while rivals play hero cards into your realm to slow you down. The asymmetry is real and the theme is executed beautifully.

5p.title: Hottest 5-Player Board Games
5p.description: Five players breaks a lot of games — odd team counts, long downtime, stretched components. These are the games that handle five gracefully and stay fun doing it.

6p-plus.title: Hottest 6+ Player Board Games
6p-plus.description: Games built to survive a full table — six or more players without collapsing into chaos or hour-long waits between turns. The bigger the group, the better these get.

games.blurb: The spymaster's constraint — one word must connect multiple targets — makes this a masterclass in lateral thinking. The whole table argues about every clue. One of the best games ever designed for a mixed group of any size.

games.title: Coup
games.blurb: Bluff your way through a corrupt dystopian court in 15 minutes. Everyone has two hidden influence cards; claim any power you want and dare someone to call you out. The best "one more game" game ever made.

games.title: Monikers
games.blurb: Three rounds with the same card deck: describe it any way, then only one word, then only gestures. By round three everyone remembers the failed descriptions from round one and loses it entirely. One of the funniest party games ever made.

games.title: Chronology
games.blurb: Build a personal timeline by correctly placing historical events in chronological order. Your timeline grows longer (and easier to place into) as you succeed — a clever self- balancing mechanic that keeps everyone competitive regardless of history knowledge.

games.title: That's Not a Hat
games.blurb: Memory meets bluffing — pass gifts around a circle, but nobody can look at what they don't hold. Accept a gift you think doesn't match what you were told, or call out the bluffer. Short, loud, and great at any age.

games.title: King of Tokyo
games.blurb: Roll Yahtzee-style dice, smash Tokyo, absorb damage, buy power cards. The risk calculus of staying in the monster-infested city long enough to score points but not so long you get eliminated is genuinely interesting in a 30-minute game.

games.title: Disney Villainous
games.blurb: Each player is an iconic Disney villain pursuing their own unique objective on their own board — while rivals play hero cards into your realm to slow you down. The asymmetry is real and the theme is executed beautifully.

games.title: Camel Up
games.blurb: Camels race around a track while physically stacking on each other — the one on top carries the rest. Bet on the leg winner, the race winner, and the loser simultaneously. Gleefully chaotic, statistically fascinating, and always funny.

games.title: 7 Wonders
games.blurb: Simultaneous card-drafting lets seven people finish a full civilization game in 30 minutes. Each player pursues their wonder's unique strategy while watching what the neighbors are building. Plays in the same time regardless of player count.

games.title: Dixit
games.blurb: Describe a dreamlike illustration with a clue that's neither too obvious nor too cryptic. The clue-giver scores only if some players guess right — if everyone gets it, no points. Beautiful, cross-generational, and consistently delightful.

games.title: Skull
games.blurb: Three rules. Infinite mind games. Place flowers and skulls face-down, then bet on how many tiles you can flip without hitting a skull. The brilliance is that you know where your own skulls are, and so does everyone watching you.

games.title: Mysterium
games.blurb: One player is a ghost communicating only through abstract vision cards; others are psychics interpreting those visions as murder clues. The gap between what the ghost means and what the psychics understand is where all the comedy and tension lives.

games.title: Telestrations
games.blurb: Telephone meets Pictionary. Draw a phrase, pass it; the next person writes what they think was drawn; the next draws what was written. By the last player, 'mermaid' has become 'toilet surfboard'. Produces some of the funniest moments in gaming.

strategy.title: Hottest Strategy Board Games
strategy.description: Games that reward planning, reading opponents, and understanding systems. Ranges from "accessible to anyone" (Ticket to Ride) to "will take years to fully solve" (Terraforming Mars). Player count and complexity noted for each.

games.title: Catan
games.blurb: The game that launched the modern board game renaissance. Negotiate, trade, and build settlements on a modular island that's different every game. Its flaws are real — longest game ever when someone leads — but its cultural impact makes it undeniably essential.

games.title: Ticket to Ride
games.blurb: Claim railway routes across North America before rivals block the paths you need. Elegant enough to teach anyone in 10 minutes, tense enough that adults play it seriously. The definitive gateway strategy game for families.

games.title: Codenames
games.blurb: The spymaster's constraint — one word must connect multiple targets — makes this a masterclass in lateral thinking. The whole table argues about every clue. One of the best games ever designed for a mixed group of any size.

games.title: Azul
games.blurb: Tile-drafting so tactically rich that tournament players study it. Drafting what you need while denying your opponent what they need is the entire game — and it works in 30 minutes with anyone from an 8-year-old to a chess grandmaster.

games.title: Wingspan
games.blurb: The game that convinced skeptics board games had grown up. Build a bird sanctuary by chaining habitat powers into an engine that feels different every play. Won the Kennerspiel des Jahres 2019 and hasn't left the bestseller charts since.

games.title: Ticket to Ride Europe
games.blurb: The European map introduces tunnels (commit to a route that might cost extra) and ferries (require locomotive wildcards). The added uncertainty makes the routing decisions more interesting than the original — many players' preferred version of the game.

games.title: Pandemic
games.blurb: The cooperative game that defined the genre. Race to cure four diseases before outbreaks cascade out of control — and they will cascade, because the board state shifts dramatically with every draw. Nothing beats the "we almost had it" feeling when you lose.

games.title: 7 Wonders Duel
games.blurb: Dense civilization strategy in 30 minutes — the rare case where the 2-player spinoff outshines the original. Three distinct win conditions mean every game ends differently, and the tension of knowing your opponent might win on science never lets up.

games.title: Othello
games.blurb: One of the most studied abstract strategy games in history. Flip your opponent's discs by surrounding them — simple to learn in two minutes, pursued at competitive levels with opening theory as deep as chess by those who go down the rabbit hole.

games.title: Terraforming Mars
games.blurb: The flagship engine-building strategy game. Manage oxygen, temperature, and ocean coverage across hundreds of project cards while corporations compete to claim Mars. Long, complex, and deeply satisfying in a way few games match.

games.title: Cascadia
games.blurb: Draft terrain hexes and wildlife tokens to build a Pacific Northwest ecosystem. The puzzle of fitting animals into their scoring patterns against a ticking token supply is deeply satisfying — calm on the surface, genuinely strategic underneath.

games.title: King of Tokyo
games.blurb: Roll Yahtzee-style dice, smash Tokyo, absorb damage, buy power cards. The risk calculus of staying in the monster-infested city long enough to score points but not so long you get eliminated is genuinely interesting in a 30-minute game.

games.title: Root
games.blurb: Four asymmetric factions — cats, birds, woodland creatures, and vagabonds — each playing by entirely different rules on the same board. The faction matchups create wildly different games every session. One of the most innovative designs of the last decade.

games.title: Camel Up
games.blurb: Camels race around a track while physically stacking on each other — the one on top carries the rest. Bet on the leg winner, the race winner, and the loser simultaneously. Gleefully chaotic, statistically fascinating, and always funny.

games.title: 7 Wonders
games.blurb: Simultaneous card-drafting lets seven people finish a full civilization game in 30 minutes. Each player pursues their wonder's unique strategy while watching what the neighbors are building. Plays in the same time regardless of player count.

games.title: Dixit
games.blurb: Describe a dreamlike illustration with a clue that's neither too obvious nor too cryptic. The clue-giver scores only if some players guess right — if everyone gets it, no points. Beautiful, cross-generational, and consistently delightful.

games.title: Skull
games.blurb: Three rules. Infinite mind games. Place flowers and skulls face-down, then bet on how many tiles you can flip without hitting a skull. The brilliance is that you know where your own skulls are, and so does everyone watching you.

games.title: Mysterium
games.blurb: One player is a ghost communicating only through abstract vision cards; others are psychics interpreting those visions as murder clues. The gap between what the ghost means and what the psychics understand is where all the comedy and tension lives.

games.title: Scythe
games.blurb: Dieselpunk Eastern Europe, 1920s. Build mechs, harvest resources, and expand territory in a game where combat is a threat more often than a reality. Stunning production values and a strategic depth that rewards patient, long-horizon play.

games.title: Splendor Duel
games.blurb: The tighter, more tactical two-player take on gem-collecting engine building. Three asymmetric win conditions and a gem board you share and fight over makes every turn a negotiation with your own long-term strategy.

coop.title: Hottest Cooperative Board Games
coop.description: Everyone wins together or everyone loses together. The best games that put the whole table against the board — no traitor mechanics, no solo paths to victory. Coordination and communication are the core skills.

games.title: Pandemic
games.blurb: The cooperative game that defined the genre. Race to cure four diseases before outbreaks cascade out of control — and they will cascade, because the board state shifts dramatically with every draw. Nothing beats the "we almost had it" feeling when you lose.

games.title: Spirit Island
games.blurb: Play as nature spirits defending a colonized island. Every spirit has a radically different power set and playstyle; the interaction between them at higher player counts creates emergent strategies that no designer could have fully predicted.

games.title: Sky Team
games.blurb: Two pilots land a plane without talking during critical phases. Place dice in shared slots, manage speed and flaps in real time, don't crash. Voted Game of the Year 2024 — the most innovative cooperative design in years.

games.title: Everdell Duo
games.blurb: The beloved forest city-builder rebuilt specifically for two, with its own asymmetric storyline and exclusive cards. The charming woodland theme meets real engine-building depth in a purpose-made two-player experience.

games.title: Mysterium
games.blurb: One player is a ghost communicating only through abstract vision cards; others are psychics interpreting those visions as murder clues. The gap between what the ghost means and what the psychics understand is where all the comedy and tension lives.

games.title: Gloomhaven
games.blurb: The most ambitious board game ever published — a 95-scenario legacy dungeon-crawl where characters permanently retire and the story world evolves from your choices. It held #1 on BoardGameGeek for years. Nothing else at this scope exists in the hobby.

games.title: Exit: The Abandoned Cabin
games.blurb: The Kennerspiel-winning escape room in a box. The whole table works through a chain of locks and riddles against the clock -- single-use and unforgettable, the purest cooperative puzzle experience you can buy.

games.title: Pandemic Legacy: Season 1
games.blurb: Twelve months of pandemic campaigns where your failures have permanent consequences — cities get stickers, characters get scars, rules change mid-game. The most acclaimed legacy game ever made and the one that made the legacy format mainstream.

games.title: Codenames Duet
games.blurb: The cooperative version where both players act as spymaster simultaneously. Your clues must contact your partner's agents and avoid theirs — since you have different information, miscommunication is guaranteed and hilarious.

games.title: Hanabi
games.blurb: Hold your cards facing OUT — you can't see them, only your teammates can. Give limited hints to help the group play fireworks in the right order. A cooperative memory game that's harder than it looks and deeply satisfying when it clicks.

social-deduction.title: Hottest Social Deduction Board Games
social-deduction.description: Games where reading people is the entire skill — hidden roles, lying, and the moment someone's bluff collapses. The information gaps between players are the mechanics.

games.title: The Chameleon
games.blurb: One player secretly is the Chameleon — they don't know the secret word but must bluff their way through. Give a clue that's not too obvious (the Chameleon will copy you) but not so cryptic that others suspect YOU. Perfect 15-minute opener.

games.title: One Night Ultimate Werewolf
games.blurb: A single night phase, a single vote. Roles shift during the night; by morning nobody is quite sure what they are. The accusations, logic, and chaos of a standard werewolf game compressed into a perfectly repeatable 10-minute package.

games.title: Coup
games.blurb: Bluff your way through a corrupt dystopian court in 15 minutes. Everyone has two hidden influence cards; claim any power you want and dare someone to call you out. The best "one more game" game ever made.

games.title: Betrayal at House on the Hill
games.blurb: Explore a haunted house tile by tile until the Haunt triggers — at which point one player becomes the traitor. Fifty different haunts mean radically different scenarios each game. Uneven but memorable in a way few games match.

games.title: Deception: Murder in Hong Kong
games.blurb: One player is the forensic scientist who knows the murderer and weapon — but can only communicate via scenario cards, not words. The murderer is hiding at the table. One of the most tense and replayable social deduction games for larger groups.

games.title: The Resistance: Avalon
games.blurb: Merlin knows who the spies are but can't say so directly. The loyal servants must identify the spies through deduction and voting — while the spies try to figure out who Merlin is and assassinate them at the end. The best social deduction game for five or more players.

games.title: Mafia: The Party Game (Deluxe Edition)
games.blurb: The original social deduction game with 38 role cards and professional production. Mafia versus Townspeople: the Mafia eliminates one citizen each night; the town votes to execute a suspect each day. The game that taught the world what hidden-role means.

games.title: Ultimate Werewolf Extreme
games.blurb: The definitive edition of the classic hidden-role social game, with a companion app to moderate. Dozens of unique roles — Seer, Witch, Bodyguard, and many more — create dramatically different games every session. Scales from a small group to a party of 25.

party.title: Hottest Party Board Games
party.description: Games that work for any crowd, any background, and any age. No rulebook required beyond "this is how you win." Every game here can be explained in under two minutes and produces genuine moments worth remembering.

games.title: Wavelength
games.blurb: A dial with a hidden target on a spectrum (like "hot ↔ cold"). Give a clue placing your clue ON the spectrum; your team argues where on the dial the target landed. The debate is the game and it's reliably funnier than it sounds.

games.title: The Chameleon
games.blurb: One player secretly is the Chameleon — they don't know the secret word but must bluff their way through. Give a clue that's not too obvious (the Chameleon will copy you) but not so cryptic that others suspect YOU. Perfect 15-minute opener.

games.title: Codenames
games.blurb: The spymaster's constraint — one word must connect multiple targets — makes this a masterclass in lateral thinking. The whole table argues about every clue. One of the best games ever designed for a mixed group of any size.

games.title: One Night Ultimate Werewolf

games.title: Wits & Wagers Party
games.blurb: Trivia where you don't need to know the answer — you bet on whose guess is closest. Scales to enormous groups, plays in 25 minutes, and rewards reading the room over obscure knowledge. The best party trivia game format yet invented.

family.title: Hottest Family Board Games
family.description: Games that work across the full age range at the table — simple enough to teach in minutes, engaging enough that adults aren't bored. Every game here has been tested by families with children from age 8 upward.

gateway.title: Hottest Gateway Board Games
gateway.description: The games that convert people who "don't like board games" into people who do. Low barrier to entry, satisfying first play, and enough depth to keep coming back. These are the games to buy for people who only know Monopoly.

games.title: Trekking the National Parks
games.blurb: Race your hikers across a gorgeous map of the US national parks, collecting trail cards and claiming sites before rivals. Accessible to families while offering real strategic decisions about when to race versus when to stockpile.
